inline_review:
commit 039340f376743635623cc7a43194d9d5b357c85f
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()

This commit moves the file_end and end_pgoff calculations before retrieving
the folio in filemap_map_pages() to prevent nr_pages from overflowing when a
concurrent truncation shrinks the inode size.

> diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
> index 242c80a0d12d..6e72f9a067cf 100644
> --- a/mm/filemap.c
> +++ b/mm/filemap.c
> @@ -3884,14 +3884,14 @@ vm_fault_t filemap_map_pages(struct vm_fault *vmf,
>  	unsigned int nr_pages = 0, folio_type;
>  	unsigned short mmap_miss = 0, mmap_miss_saved;
>  
> +	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;

Could this new placement of the file_end calculation cause an integer underflow
when the file size is 0?

If i_size_read() returns 0, DIV_ROUND_UP() evaluates to 0. Subtracting 1
causes the unsigned pgoff_t file_end to underflow to ULONG_MAX.

> +	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);

This makes the subsequent evaluation of end_pgoff unconstrained.

If a concurrent thread extends the file (e.g., writing 1 byte) and instantiates
a large folio just before next_uptodate_folio() is called, the code might
observe the new i_size > 0 and return the large folio.

Because file_end underflowed to ULONG_MAX, the check
file_end >= folio_next_index(folio) would erroneously evaluate to true.

This could result in fully mapping the large folio (e.g., establishing a
512-page PMD via filemap_map_pmd() or full PTEs via filemap_map_folio_range())
far beyond the new 1-byte EOF.

Since the file was expanded rather than truncated, no concurrent
truncate_pagecache() will run to unmap these excess pages. Does this allow
userspace to read and write memory beyond EOF indefinitely without encountering
a SIGBUS?

[ ... ]

commit 039340f376743635623cc7a43194d9d5b357c85f
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>
Date:   Fri Mar 13 03:45:48 2026 +0000

    mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()
    
    When running stress-ng on my Arm64 machine with v7.0-rc3 kernel, I encountered
    some very strange crash issues showing up as "Bad page state":
    
    "
    [  734.496287] BUG: Bad page state in process stress-ng-env  pfn:415735fb
    [  734.496427] page: refcount:0 mapcount:1 mapping:0000000000000000 index:0x4cf316 pfn:0x415735fb
    [  734.496434] flags: 0x57fffe000000800(owner_2|node=1|zone=2|lastcpupid=0x3ffff)
    [  734.496439] raw: 057fffe000000800 0000000000000000 dead000000000122 0000000000000000
    [  734.496440] raw: 00000000004cf316 0000000000000000 0000000000000000 0000000000000000
    [  734.496442] page dumped because: nonzero mapcount
    "
    
    After analyzing this page’s state, it is hard to understand why the mapcount
    is not 0 while the refcount is 0, since this page is not where the issue first
    occurred. By enabling the CONFIG_DEBUG_VM config, I can reproduce the crash as
    well and captured the first warning where the issue appears:
    
    "
    [  734.469226] page: refcount:33 mapcount:0 mapping:00000000bef2d187 index:0x81a0 pfn:0x415735c0
    [  734.469304] head: order:5 mapcount:0 entire_mapcount:0 nr_pages_mapped:0 pincount:0
    [  734.469315] memcg:ffff000807a8ec00
    [  734.469320] aops:ext4_da_aops ino:100b6f dentry name(?):"stress-ng-mmaptorture-9397-0-2736200540"
    [  734.469335] flags: 0x57fffe400000069(locked|uptodate|lru|head|node=1|zone=2|lastcpupid=0x3ffff)
    ......
    [  734.469364] page dumped because: VM_WARN_ON_FOLIO((_Generic((page + nr_pages - 1),
    const struct page *: (const struct folio *)_compound_head(page + nr_pages - 1), struct page *:
    (struct folio *)_compound_head(page + nr_pages - 1))) != folio)
    [  734.469390] ------------[ cut here ]------------
    [  734.469393] WARNING: ./include/linux/rmap.h:351 at folio_add_file_rmap_ptes+0x3b8/0x468,
    CPU#90: stress-ng-mlock/9430
    [  734.469551]  folio_add_file_rmap_ptes+0x3b8/0x468 (P)
    [  734.469555]  set_pte_range+0xd8/0x2f8
    [  734.469566]  filemap_map_folio_range+0x190/0x400
    [  734.469579]  filemap_map_pages+0x348/0x638
    [  734.469583]  do_fault_around+0x140/0x198
    ......
    [  734.469640]  el0t_64_sync+0x184/0x188
    "
    
    The code that triggers the warning is: "VM_WARN_ON_FOLIO(page_folio(page + nr_pages - 1) != folio, folio)",
    which indicates that set_pte_range() tried to map beyond the large folio’s
    size.
    
    By adding more debug information, I found that 'nr_pages' had overflowed in
    filemap_map_pages(), causing set_pte_range() to establish mappings for a range
    exceeding the folio size, potentially corrupting fields of pages that do not
    belong to this folio (e.g., page->_mapcount).
    
    After above analysis, I think the possible race is as follows:
    
    CPU 0                                                  CPU 1
    filemap_map_pages()                                   ext4_setattr()
       //get and lock folio with old inode->i_size
       next_uptodate_folio()
    
                                                              .......
                                                              //shrink the inode->i_size
                                                              i_size_write(inode, attr->ia_size);
    
       //calculate the end_pgoff with the new inode->i_size
       file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
       end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
    
       ......
       //nr_pages can be overflowed, cause xas.xa_index > end_pgoff
       end = folio_next_index(folio) - 1;
       nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1;
    
       ......
       //map large folio
       filemap_map_folio_range()
                                                              ......
                                                              //truncate folios
                                                              truncate_pagecache(inode, inode->i_size);
    
    To fix this issue, move the 'end_pgoff' calculation before next_uptodate_folio(),
    so the retrieved folio stays consistent with the file end to avoid 'nr_pages'
    calculation overflow. After this patch, the crash issue is gone.
    
    Fixes: 743a2753a02e ("filemap: cap PTE range to be created to allowed zero fill in folio_map_range()")
    Reported-by: Yuanhe Shu <xiangzao@linux.alibaba.com>
    Tested-by: Yuanhe Shu <xiangzao@linux.alibaba.com>
    Signed-off-by: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
index 242c80a0d12d..6e72f9a067cf 100644
--- a/mm/filemap.c
+++ b/mm/filemap.c
@@ -3884,14 +3884,14 @@ vm_fault_t filemap_map_pages(struct vm_fault *vmf,
 	unsigned int nr_pages = 0, folio_type;
 	unsigned short mmap_miss = 0, mmap_miss_saved;
 
+	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
+	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
+
 	rcu_read_lock();
 	folio = next_uptodate_folio(&xas, mapping, end_pgoff);
 	if (!folio)
 		goto out;
 
-	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
-	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
-
 	/*
 	 * Do not allow to map with PMD across i_size to preserve
 	 * SIGBUS semantics.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Large Folio Mapping Issue**

Okay, here's the deal. I've been dissecting this commit message about an overflow issue within `filemap_map_pages()` that's triggered during folio mapping, particularly when dealing with large folios and concurrent truncation. The heart of the problem seems to be a race condition, specifically when a process like `stress-ng` tries to map pages of a file while another process truncates that file.

The core of the issue is in how `nr_pages` is calculated. This unsigned integer gets a wildly large value because of an integer overflow, leading to out-of-bounds mapping. This stems from a scenario where `i_size` (the file size) is modified concurrently. CPU 0 reads the file size at one point, calculates the number of pages, then a truncation happens on CPU 1. This invalidates the previously calculated `nr_pages` and leads to mapping pages beyond the truncated file size.

The original fix moves the calculation of `file_end` (the end page offset) to happen *before* getting the folio. I was initially concerned about calculating `file_end` only once, as this value might be stale if the file gets truncated in the middle of a loop that iterates through pages. If it is calculated at the beginning of the function, it won't be updated when mapping each page in the loop. This means we might have to re-evaluate it within the page mapping loop.

However, the real danger is mapping a folio that has been truncated! If the page cache is truncated *after* `file_end` is read but *before* we map a folio, then we are mapping a stale folio.

After careful consideration of the sequence of operations with `truncate_pagecache`, `unmap_mapping_range`, and the interactions with the `pte_lock`, I realized that the real problem lies in the fact that `filemap_map_pages` reads `i_size` *before* the critical `next_uptodate_folio()` call. The `next_uptodate_folio` does the actual folio locking.

The fix introduced a data race. If CPU 1 is in the middle of truncating the file, the following sequence is possible:
1. CPU 0 reads `i_size` (OLD size), which is used to calculate `file_end` before a truncation happens
2. CPU 1 is truncating, unmapping the page with `unmap_mapping_range`
3. CPU 0 then tries to read `next_uptodate_folio` which, if the `i_size` is read and checked, will see if we are in this scenario
4. CPU 0 proceeds and maps folio 20 into PTE.
5. CPU 0 unlocks the folio
6. CPU 1 locks the folio
7. Folio gets removed from the page cache

So, it's possible to create a PTE mapping for a page that's already been truncated, thus, leading to stale PTEs. The page will be truncated but remain mapped in a page table, ready for a use-after-free, or at least a stale page.

The real key here is that, to avoid stale pages, the read of `i_size` must happen *after* we've locked the folio. This way, we always see the most up-to-date `i_size`, and we can safely abort if the page has been truncated. The page fault handling already does this check.

Therefore, the original fix is INCORRECT. We must read the `i_size` AFTER obtaining the folio lock. It is critical to synchronize the page mapping with the concurrent truncation, and this is why `filemap_fault` reads `i_size` AFTER folio locking. This ensures we never map pages beyond the current file size.

Possible data corruption due to collision with buffered I/O!\\n\");\n\t\tpr_crit(\"File: %s PID: %d Comm: %.20s\\n\", path, current->pid,\n\t\t\tcurrent->comm);\n\t}\n}\n\nvoid kiocb_invalidate_post_direct_write(struct kiocb *iocb, size_t count)\n{\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = iocb->ki_filp->f_mapping;\n\n\tif (mapping->nrpages &&\n\t    invalidate_inode_pages2_range(mapping,\n\t\t\tiocb->ki_pos >> PAGE_SHIFT,\n\t\t\t(iocb->ki_pos + count - 1) >> PAGE_SHIFT))\n\t\tdio_warn_stale_pagecache(iocb->ki_filp);\n}\n\nssize_t\ngeneric_file_direct_write(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)\n{\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = iocb->ki_filp->f_mapping;\n\tsize_t write_len = iov_iter_count(from);\n\tssize_t written;\n\n\t/*\n\t * If a page can not be invalidated, return 0 to fall back\n\t * to buffered write.\n\t */\n\twritten = kiocb_invalidate_pages(iocb, write_len);\n\tif (written) {\n\t\tif (written == -EBUSY)\n\t\t\treturn 0;\n\t\treturn written;\n\t}\n\n\twritten = mapping->a_ops->direct_IO(iocb, from);\n\n\t/*\n\t * Finally, try again to invalidate clean pages which might have been\n\t * cached by non-direct readahead, or faulted in by get_user_pages()\n\t * if the source of the write was an mmap'ed region of the file\n\t * we're writing. It takes care of syncing the file in case of O_SYNC file\n * and acquires i_rwsem as needed.\n * Return:\n * * negative error code if no data has been written at all of\n *   vfs_fsync_range() failed for a synchronous write\n * * number of bytes written, even for truncated writes\n */\nssize_t generic_file_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)\n{\n\tstruct file *file = iocb->ki_filp;\n\tstruct inode *inode = file->f_mapping->host;\n\tssize_t ret;\n\n\tinode_lock(inode);\n\tret = generic_write_checks(iocb, from);\n\tif (ret > 0)\n\t\tret = __generic_file_write_iter(iocb, from);\n\tinode_unlock(inode);\n\n\tif (ret > 0)\n\t\tret = generic_write_sync(iocb, ret);\n\treturn ret;\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(generic_file_write_iter);\n\n/**\n * filemap_release_folio() - struct folio *do_read_cache_folio(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\tpgoff_t index, filler_t filler, struct file *file, gfp_t gfp)\n{\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\tint err;\n\n\tif (!filler)\n\t\tfiller = mapping->a_ops->read_folio;\nrepeat:\n\tfolio = filemap_get_folio(mapping, index);\n\tif (IS_ERR(folio)) {\n\t\tfolio = filemap_alloc_folio(gfp, mapping_min_folio_order(mapping), NULL);\n\t\tif (!folio)\n\t\t\treturn ERR_PTR(-ENOMEM);\n\t\tindex = mapping_align_index(mapping, index);\n\t\terr = filemap_add_folio(mapping, folio, index, gfp);\n\t\tif (unlikely(err)) (folio_test_uptodate(folio))\n\t\tgoto out;\n\n\tif (!folio_trylock(folio)) {\n\t\tfolio_put_wait_locked(folio, TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE);\n\t\tgoto repeat;\n\t}\n\n\t/* Folio was truncated from mapping */\n\tif (!folio->mapping) {\n\t\tfolio_unlock(folio);\n\t\tfolio_put(folio);\n\t\tgoto repeat;\n\t}\n\n\t/* Someone else locked and filled the page in a very small window */\n\tif (folio_test_uptodate(folio)) {\n\t\tfolio_unlock(folio);\n\t\tgoto out;\n\t}\n\nfiller:\n\terr = filemap_read_folio(file, filler, folio);\n\tif (err) {\n\t\tfolio_put(folio);\n\t\tif (err == AOP_TRUNCATED_PAGE)\n\t\t\tgoto repeat;\n\t\treturn ERR_PTR(err);\n\t}\n\nout:\n\tfolio_mark_accessed(folio);\n\treturn folio;\n}\n\n/**\n * read_cache_folio - Read into page cache, fill it if Expects mapping->invalidate_lock to be held.\n * Return: An uptodate folio on success, ERR_PTR() on failure.\n */\nstruct folio *read_cache_folio(struct address_space *mapping, pgoff_t index,\n\t\tfiller_t filler, struct file *file)\n{\n\treturn do_read_cache_folio(mapping, index, filler, file,\n\t\t\tmapping_gfp_mask(mapping));\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(read_cache_folio);\n\n/**\n * mapping_read_folio_gfp - Read into page cache, using specified allocation flags.\n * @mapping:\tThe address_space for the folio.\n * @index:\tThe index that the allocated folio will contain.\n * @gfp:\tThe page allocator flags to use if allocating.\n *\n * This is the same as \"read_cache_folio(mapping, index, NULL, NULL)\", but with\n * any new memory allocations done using the specified allocation flags.\n *\n * The most likely error from this function is EIO, but ENOMEM is\n * possible and so is EINTR. read into page cache, using specified page allocation flags.\n * @mapping:\tthe page's address_space\n * @index:\tthe page index\n * @gfp:\tthe page allocator flags to use if allocating\n *\n * This is the same as \"read_mapping_page(mapping, index, NULL)\", but with\n * any new page allocations done using the specified allocation flags.\n *\n * If the page does not get brought uptodate, return -EIO.\n *\n * The function expects mapping->invalidate_lock to be already held.\n *\n * Return: up to date page on success, ERR_PTR() on failure.\n */\nstruct page *read_cache_page_gfp(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\t\t\tpgoff_t index,\n\t\t\t\tgfp_t gfp)\n{\n\treturn do_read_cache_page(mapping, index, NULL, NULL, gfp);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(read_cache_page_gfp);\n\n/*\n * Warn about a page cache invalidation failure during a direct I/O write.\n */\nstatic void dio_warn_stale_pagecache(struct file *filp)\n{\n\tstatic DEFINE_RATELIMIT_STATE(_rs, 86400 * HZ, DEFAULT_RATELIMIT_BURST);\n\tchar pathname[128];\n\tchar *path;\n\n\terrseq_set(&filp->f_mapping->wb_err, -EIO);\n\tif (__ratelimit(&_rs)) {\n\t\tpath = file_path(filp, pathname, sizeof(pathname));\n\t\tif (IS_ERR(path))\n\t\t\tpath = \"(unknown)\";\n\t\tpr_crit(\"Page cache invalidation failure on direct I/O.  Possible data corruption due to collision with buffered I/O!\\n\");\n\t\tpr_crit(\"File: %s PID: %d Comm: %.20s\\n\", path, current->pid,\n\t\t\tcurrent->comm);\n\t}\n}\n\nvoid kiocb_invalidate_post_direct_write(struct kiocb *iocb, size_t count)\n{\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = iocb->ki_filp->f_mapping;\n\n\tif (mapping->nrpages &&\n\t    invalidate_inode_pages2_range(mapping,\n\t\t\tiocb->ki_pos >> PAGE_SHIFT,\n\t\t\t(iocb->ki_pos + count - 1) >> PAGE_SHIFT))\n\t\tdio_warn_stale_pagecache(iocb->ki_filp);\n}\n\nssize_t\ngeneric_file_direct_write(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)\n{\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = iocb->ki_filp->f_mapping;\n\tsize_t write_len = iov_iter_count(from);\n\tssize_t written;\n\n\t/*\n\t * If a page can not be invalidated, return 0 to fall back\n\t * to buffered write.\n\t */\n\twritten = kiocb_invalidate_pages(iocb, write_len);\n\tif (written) {\n\t\tif (written == -EBUSY)\n\t\t\treturn 0;\n\t\treturn written;\n\t}\n\n\twritten = mapping->a_ops->direct_IO(iocb, from);\n\n\t/*\n\t * Finally, try again to invalidate clean pages which might have been\n\t * cached by non-direct readahead, or faulted in by get_user_pages()\n\t * if the source of the write was an mmap'ed region of the file\n\t * we're writing. iov_iter_count(from));\n\treturn written;\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(generic_file_direct_write);\n\nssize_t generic_perform_write(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *i)\n{\n\tstruct file *file = iocb->ki_filp;\n\tloff_t pos = iocb->ki_pos;\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = file->f_mapping;\n\tconst struct address_space_operations *a_ops = mapping->a_ops;\n\tsize_t chunk = mapping_max_folio_size(mapping);\n\tlong status = 0;\n\tssize_t written = 0;\n\n\tdo {\n\t\tstruct folio *folio;\n\t\tsize_t offset;\t\t/* Offset into folio */\n\t\tsize_t bytes;\t\t/* Bytes to write to folio */\n\t\tsize_t copied;\t\t/* Bytes copied from user */\n\t\tvoid *fsdata = NULL;\n\n\t\tbytes = iov_iter_count(i);\nretry:\n\t\toffset = pos & (chunk - 1);\n\t\tbytes = min(chunk - offset, bytes);\n\t\tbalance_dirty_pages_ratelimited(mapping);\n\n\t\tif (fatal_signal_pending(current)) {\n\t\t\tstatus = -EINTR;\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tstatus = a_ops->write_begin(iocb, mapping, pos, bytes,\n\t\t\t\t\t\t&folio, &fsdata);\n\t\tif (unlikely(status < 0))\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\toffset = offset_in_folio(folio, pos);\n\t\tif (bytes > folio_size(folio) - offset)\n\t\t\tbytes = folio_size(folio) - offset;\n\n\t\tif (mapping_writably_mapped(mapping))\n\t\t\tflush_dcache_folio(folio);\n\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Faults here on mmap()s can recurse into arbitrary\n\t\t * filesystem code. It does all basic checks, removes SUID from the file, updates\n * modification times and calls proper subroutines depending on whether we\n * do direct IO or a standard buffered write.\n *\n * It expects i_rwsem to be grabbed unless we work on a block device or similar\n * object which does not need locking at all.\n *\n * This function does *not* take care of syncing data in case of O_SYNC write.\n * A caller has to handle it. This is mainly due to the fact that we want to\n * avoid syncing under i_rwsem.\n *\n * Return:\n * * number of bytes written, even for truncated writes\n * * negative error code if no data has been written at all\n */\nssize_t __generic_file_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)\n{\n\tstruct file *file = iocb->ki_filp;\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = file->f_mapping;\n\tstruct inode *inode = mapping->host;\n\tssize_t ret;\n\n\tret = file_remove_privs(file);\n\tif (ret)\n\t\treturn ret;\n\n\tret = file_update_time(file);\n\tif (ret)\n\t\treturn ret;\n\n\tif (iocb->ki_flags & IOCB_DIRECT) {\n\t\tret = generic_file_direct_write(iocb, from);\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * If the write stopped short of completing, fall back to\n\t\t * buffered writes.  Some filesystems do this for writes to\n\t\t * holes, for example.  For DAX files, a buffered write will\n\t\t * not succeed (even if it did, DAX does not handle dirty\n\t\t * page-cache pages correctly).\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (ret < 0 || !iov_iter_count(from) || IS_DAX(inode))\n\t\t\treturn ret;\n\t\treturn direct_write_fallback(iocb, from, ret,\n\t\t\t\tgeneric_perform_write(iocb, from));\n\t}\n\n\treturn generic_perform_write(iocb, from);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(__generic_file_write_iter);\n\n/**\n * generic_file_write_iter - write data to a file\n * @iocb:\tIO state structure\n * @from:\tiov_iter with data to write\n *\n * This is a wrapper around __generic_file_write_iter() to be used by most\n * filesystems. It takes care of syncing the file in case of O_SYNC file\n * and acquires i_rwsem as needed.\n * Return:\n * * negative error code if no data has been written at all of\n *   vfs_fsync_range() failed for a synchronous write\n * * number of bytes written, even for truncated writes\n */\nssize_t generic_file_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)\n{\n\tstruct file *file = iocb->ki_filp;\n\tstruct inode *inode = file->f_mapping->host;\n\tssize_t ret;\n\n\tinode_lock(inode);\n\tret = generic_write_checks(iocb, from);\n\tif (ret > 0)\n\t\tret = __generic_file_write_iter(iocb, from);\n\tinode_unlock(inode);\n\n\tif (ret > 0)\n\t\tret = generic_write_sync(iocb, ret);\n\treturn ret;\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(generic_file_write_iter);\n\n/**\n * filemap_release_folio() - Release fs-specific metadata on a folio.\n * @folio: The folio which the kernel is trying to free.\n * @gfp: Memory allocation flags (and I/O mode).\n *\n * The address_space is trying to release any data attached to a folio\n * (presumably at folio->private).\n *\n * This will also be called if the private_2 flag is set on a page,\n * indicating that the folio has other metadata associated with it.\n *\n * The @gfp argument specifies whether I/O may be performed to release\n * this page (__GFP_IO), and whether the call may block\n * (__GFP_RECLAIM & __GFP_FS).\n *\n * Return: %true if the release was successful, otherwise %false.\n */\nbool filemap_release_folio(struct folio *folio, gfp_t gfp)\n{\n\tstruct address_space * const mapping = folio->mapping;\n\n\tBUG_ON(!folio_test_locked(folio));\n\tif (!folio_needs_release(folio))\n\t\treturn true;\n\tif (folio_test_writeback(folio))\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tif (mapping && mapping->a_ops->release_folio)\n\t\treturn mapping->a_ops->release_folio(folio, gfp);\n\treturn try_to_free_buffers(folio);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(filemap_release_folio);\n\n/**\n * filemap_invalidate_inode - Invalidate/forcibly write back a range of an inode's pagecache\n * @inode: The inode to flush\n * @flush: Set to write back rather than simply invalidate.\n * @start: First byte to in range.\n * @end: Last byte in range (inclusive), or LLONG_MAX for everything from start\n *       onwards.\n *\n * Invalidate all the folios on an inode that contribute to the specified\n * range, possibly writing them back first.  Whilst the operation is\n * undertaken, the invalidate lock is held to prevent new folios from being\n * installed.\n */\nint filemap_invalidate_inode(struct inode *inode, bool flush,\n\t\t\t     loff_t start, loff_t end)\n{\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = inode->i_mapping;\n\tpgoff_t first = start >> PAGE_SHIFT;\n\tpgoff_t last = end >> PAGE_SHIFT;\n\tpgoff_t nr = end == LLONG_MAX ? ULONG_MAX : last - first + 1;\n\n\tif (!mapping || !mapping->nrpages || end < start)\n\t\tgoto out;\n\n\t/* Prevent new folios from being added to the inode. */\n\tfilemap_invalidate_lock(mapping);\n\n\tif (!mapping->nrpages)\n\t\tgoto unlock;\n\n\tunmap_mapping_pages(mapping, first, nr, false);\n\n\t/* Write back the data if we're asked to. */\n\tif (flush)\n\t\tfilemap_fdatawrite_range(mapping, start, end);\n\n\t/* Wait for writeback to complete on all folios and discard. */\n\tinvalidate_inode_pages2_range(mapping, start / PAGE_SIZE, end / PAGE_SIZE);\n\nunlock:\n\tfilemap_invalidate_unlock(mapping);\nout:\n\treturn filemap_check_errors(mapping);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(filemap_invalidate_inode);\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_CACHESTAT_SYSCALL\n/**\n * filemap_cachestat() - compute the page cache statistics of a mapping\n * @mapping:\tThe mapping to compute the statistics for.\n * @first_index:\tThe starting page cache index.\n * @last_index:\tThe final page index (inclusive).\n * @cs:\tthe cachestat struct to write the result to.\n *\n * This will query the page cache statistics of a mapping in the\n * page range of [first_index, last_index] (inclusive). The statistics\n * queried include: number of dirty pages, number of pages marked for\n * writeback, and the number of (recently) evicted pages.\n */\nstatic void filemap_cachestat(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\tpgoff_t first_index, pgoff_t last_index, struct cachestat *cs)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, first_index);\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\n\t/* Flush stats (and potentially sleep) outside the RCU read section. */\n\tmem_cgroup_flush_stats_ratelimited(NULL);\n\n\trcu_read_lock();\n\txas_for_each(&xas, folio, last_index) {\n\t\tint order;\n\t\tunsigned long nr_pages;\n\t\tpgoff_t folio_first_index, folio_last_index;\n\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Don't deref the folio. It is not pinned, and might\n\t\t * get freed (and reused) underneath us.\n\t\t *\n\t\t * We *could* pin it, but that would be expensive for\n\t\t * what should be a fast and lightweight syscall.\n\t\t *\n\t\t * Instead, derive all information of interest from\n\t\t * the rcu-protected xarray.\n\t\t */\n\n\t\tif (xas_retry(&xas, folio))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\n\t\torder = xas_get_order(&xas);\n\t\tnr_pages = 1 << order;\n\t\tfolio_first_index = round_down(xas.xa_index, 1 << order);\n\t\tfolio_last_index = folio_first_index + nr_pages - 1;\n\n\t\t/* Folios might straddle the range boundaries, only count covered pages */\n\t\tif (folio_first_index < first_index)\n\t\t\tnr_pages -= first_index - folio_first_index;\n\n\t\tif (folio_last_index > last_index)\n\t\t\tnr_pages -= folio_last_index - last_index;\n\n\t\tif (xa_is_value(folio)) {\n\t\t\t/* page is evicted */\n\t\t\tvoid *shadow = (void *)folio;\n\t\t\tbool workingset; /* not used */\n\n\t\t\tcs->nr_evicted += nr_pages;\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_SWAP /* implies CONFIG_MMU */\n\t\t\tif (shmem_mapping(mapping)) {\n\t\t\t\t/* shmem file - in swap cache */\n\t\t\t\tswp_entry_t swp = radix_to_swp_entry(folio);\n\n\t\t\t\t/* swapin error results in poisoned entry */\n\t\t\t\tif (!softleaf_is_swap(swp))\n\t\t\t\t\tgoto resched;\n\n\t\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t\t * Getting a swap entry from the shmem\n\t\t\t\t * inode means we beat\n\t\t\t\t * shmem_unuse(). rcu_read_lock()\n\t\t\t\t * ensures swapoff waits for us before\n\t\t\t\t * freeing the swapper space. However,\n\t\t\t\t * we can race with swapping and\n\t\t\t\t * invalidation, so there might not be\n\t\t\t\t * a shadow in the swapcache (yet).\n\t\t\t\t */\n\t\t\t\tshadow = swap_cache_get_shadow(swp);\n\t\t\t\tif (!shadow)\n\t\t\t\t\tgoto resched;\n\t\t\t}\n#endif\n\t\t\tif (workingset_test_recent(shadow, true, &workingset, false))\n\t\t\t\tcs->nr_recently_evicted += nr_pages;\n\n\t\t\tgoto resched;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\t/* page is in cache */\n\t\tcs->nr_cache += nr_pages;\n\n\t\tif (xas_get_mark(&xas, PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY))\n\t\t\tcs->nr_dirty += nr_pages;\n\n\t\tif (xas_get_mark(&xas, PAGECACHE_TAG_WRITEBACK))\n\t\t\tcs->nr_writeback += nr_pages;\n\nresched:\n\t\tif (need_resched()) {\n\t\t\txas_pause(&xas);\n\t\t\tcond_resched_rcu();\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\trcu_read_unlock();\n}\n\n/*\n * See mincore: reveal pagecache information only for files\n * that the calling process has write access to, or could (if\n * tried) open for writing.\n */\nstatic inline bool can_do_cachestat(struct file *f)\n{\n\tif (f->f_mode & FMODE_WRITE)\n\t\treturn true;\n\tif (inode_owner_or_capable(file_mnt_idmap(f), file_inode(f)))\n\t\treturn true;\n\treturn file_permission(f, MAY_WRITE) == 0;\n}\n\n/*\n * The cachestat(2) system call.\n *\n * cachestat() returns the page cache statistics of a file in the\n * bytes range specified by `off` and `len`: number of cached pages,\n * number of dirty pages, number of pages marked for writeback,\n * number of evicted pages, and number of recently evicted pages.\n *\n * An evicted page is a page that is previously in the page cache\n * but has been evicted since.     "thought": "**Analyzing the Race Condition in `filemap_map_pages`**

Okay, so the core issue is an unsigned integer overflow in `nr_pages` calculation within `filemap_map_pages` due to a race condition with file truncation. The original code's calculation of `nr_pages` depended on `i_size` which could change concurrently, leading to an underflow and massive value assigned to `nr_pages`. My job is to see if the proposed patch actually fixes the problem.

The original code's issue was that it calculated the upper bound for the page mapping (`end_pgoff`) *after* potentially grabbing a folio with `next_uptodate_folio`. This meant if the file was truncated *between* those two steps, the calculated upper bound could be smaller than the folio's index, causing `nr_pages` to underflow.  The proposed patch rearranges this: calculate the upper bound *first*.

So, I had to trace a few potential race scenarios:

1.  **Truncation *before* `next_uptodate_folio`:** If the file truncates *after* `end_pgoff` is calculated, but *before* the call to `next_uptodate_folio`, the upper bound would be larger than the new file size. Then, `next_uptodate_folio` would still find the folio because we calculate `end_pgoff` *first* based on the *old* `i_size`. The resulting `nr_pages` calculation should be fine, and no underflow would happen. So the page map will still be created, but it maps beyond the new size, potentially creating a temporary mapping beyond the current size. The important point is whether this is dangerous.

2.  **Mapping Beyond the New `i_size`:** I had to determine if mapping beyond the new file size created any problems. I realized that a subsequent truncation (`truncate_pagecache`) would clean up any such mappings by iterating and releasing folios and PTEs. I needed to see how these operations interact. I had to look at `truncate_pagecache`, especially how it interacts with the page cache entries and page tables.  It will first change `inode->i_size` then truncate the page cache. I had to determine what actions are taken at each step.

3.  **Folio Locking and Concurrency:** I had to see if folio locking and concurrent access would cause problems. It became clear that `next_uptodate_folio` *locks* the folio it returns. Thus, I could prove that `truncate_pagecache` would be blocked by the folio lock held by `filemap_map_pages`, so any PTE installation performed by `filemap_map_pages` cannot be interrupted by `truncate_pagecache` during the page cache removal. This makes this safe, because `filemap_map_pages` could still establish a mapping that might later be destroyed by a subsequent call to `truncate_pagecache`.

4.  **`filemap_map_folio_range` and `file_end`:** I had to verify what `filemap_map_folio_range` does, and what is its relation with `file_end`, since this function is called inside the loop of the `filemap_map_pages` function. It turned out that the old value `file_end` (which is calculated before the loop), if larger than the new `i_size`, will not cause the function to go OOB, but will cause it to miss fault-around mapping, which is an optimization anyway.

5.  **PMD Mapping and Partial Installation:** I then had to see what happens when we map with PMD. `filemap_map_pmd` is the core function for mapping a whole PMD (1GB). It turns out that `filemap_map_pmd` holds the folio lock *while* installing the PMD. So the truncation will be blocked until the PMD has been installed, then uninstalled, so the PMD would not be torn down before it is installed.

6.  **Edge Case: Truncation Beyond Existing Folio:** I then considered the scenario where `next_uptodate_folio` returns a folio *completely* beyond the new file size. I realized that if the file had been truncated *before* `next_uptodate_folio`, the folio would have been removed from the page cache, and `next_uptodate_folio` wouldn't find it to begin with.

When we arrive at `filemap_map_pages`, `end_pgoff` uses the *old* `i_size` to determine the mapping extent and the potential issue would be the `min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1` computation might produce an integer overflow. Given the bounds, this is not possible because `xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff`. So that's safe.

The real concern is whether we map folios *past* the new `i_size`. The `truncate_pagecache` function is responsible for adjusting the page cache to reflect the new `i_size`.  I realized `truncate_pagecache` will call `unmap_mapping_range` and remove folios. The critical point is that since `truncate_pagecache` locks the folio to remove it and `filemap_map_pages` locks the folio in `next_uptodate_folio`, the truncation will block until `filemap_map_pages` has finished its execution. Then, `truncate_pagecache` acquires the folio lock, then calls `unmap_mapping_folio` (or a range unmap), tearing down the PTEs.

Does `truncate_pagecache` *completely* tear down PTEs of the pages that straddle the boundary? I remembered that `truncate_pagecache` -> `truncate_inode_pages_range` would partially-truncate the folio. This process zeros the tail end, but it's not clear whether the PTEs are all removed. If the PTEs remain, the mapping process would result in userspace getting all zeros. And mapping beyond the new `i_size` could cause SIGBUSes.

Fortunately, the `next_uptodate_folio` function's check `xas->xa_index >= max_idx` ensures that only folios with a start index *before* or *straddling* the new `i_size` are returned. This prevents mapping folios *completely* beyond the new `i_size`.

Then I considered large folios (THPs). Although `xa_index` might be valid, a large folio could span multiple pages beyond `new_i_size`. The concern here is whether `truncate_pagecache` correctly handles such cases.

Tracing `truncate_pagecache` for large folios reveals that `truncate_inode_pages_range` calls `truncate_inode_partial_folio`, which will first lock the folio. Then, after zero-filling, it *splits* the folio, *removes the tail pages from the page cache*, and *UNMAPS* the tail pages! The critical thing I noticed is that `unmap_mapping_range` happens *before* the removal of folios from the page cache.

This leads to a race condition. If one CPU runs `unmap_mapping_range` it might unmap PTEs. But another CPU might be in `filemap_map_pages`, has acquired the folio lock, and is establishing *new* PTEs! And, does `unmap_mapping_range` wait for the folio lock? No, because it works on the page tables (ptl). So CPU 1 could unmap the PTEs and then attempt to remove folios, blocking on the folio lock. Meanwhile, CPU 0, holding the folio lock, is setting up NEW PTEs. Then, CPU 0 releases the lock, CPU 1 gets it, zeroes the partial page, *removes* the tail pages. But, does it unmap the *newly-created* PTEs from CPU 0? I need to verify that.

    "content": "{"content":"// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0-only\n/*\n * mm/truncate.c - code for taking down pages from address_spaces\n *\n * Copyright (C) 2002, Linus Torvalds\n *\n * 10Sep2002\tAndrew Morton\n *\t\tInitial version.\n */\n\n#include <linux/kernel.h>\n#include <linux/backing-dev.h>\n#include <linux/dax.h>\n#include <linux/gfp.h>\n#include <linux/mm.h>\n#include <linux/swap.h>\n#include <linux/export.h>\n#include <linux/pagemap.h>\n#include <linux/highmem.h>\n#include <linux/folio_batch.h>\n#include <linux/task_io_accounting_ops.h>\n#include <linux/shmem_fs.h>\n#include <linux/rmap.h>\n#include \"internal.h\"\n\nstatic void clear_shadow_entries(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\t\t\t unsigned long start, unsigned long max)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, start);\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\n\t/* Handled by shmem itself, or for DAX we do nothing. */\n\tif (shmem_mapping(mapping) || dax_mapping(mapping))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\txas_set_update(&xas, workingset_update_node);\n\n\tspin_lock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\n\txas_lock_irq(&xas);\n\n\t/* Clear all shadow entries from start to max */\n\txas_for_each(&xas, folio, max) {\n\t\tif (xa_is_value(folio))\n\t\t\txas_store(&xas, NULL);\n\t}\n\n\txas_unlock_irq(&xas);\n\tif (mapping_shrinkable(mapping))\n\t\tinode_lru_list_add(mapping->host);\n\tspin_unlock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\n}\n\n/*\n * Unconditionally remove exceptional entries. Usually called from truncate\n * path. Note that the folio_batch may be altered by this function by removing\n * exceptional entries similar to what folio_batch_remove_exceptionals() does.\n * Please note that indices[] has entries in ascending order as guaranteed by\n * either find_get_entries() or find_lock_entries().\n */\nstatic void truncate_folio_batch_exceptionals(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\t\t\tstruct folio_batch *fbatch, pgoff_t *indices)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, indices[0]);\n\tint nr = folio_batch_count(fbatch);\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\tint i, j;\n\n\t/* Handled by shmem itself */\n\tif (shmem_mapping(mapping))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tfor (j = 0; j < nr; j++)\n\t\tif (xa_is_value(fbatch->folios[j]))\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\tif (j == nr)\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tif (dax_mapping(mapping)) {\n\t\tfor (i = j; i < nr; i++) {\n\t\t\tif (xa_is_value(fbatch->folios[i])) {\n\t\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t\t * File systems should already have called\n\t\t\t\t * dax_break_layout_entry() to remove all DAX\n\t\t\t\t * entries while holding a lock to prevent\n\t\t\t\t * establishing new entries. Therefore we\n\t\t\t\t * shouldn't find any here.\n\t\t\t\t */\n\t\t\t\tWARN_ON_ONCE(1);\n\n\t\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t\t * Delete the mapping so truncate_pagecache()\n\t\t\t\t * doesn't loop forever.\n\t\t\t\t */\n\t\t\t\tdax_delete_mapping_entry(mapping, indices[i]);\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t\tgoto out;\n\t}\n\n\txas_set(&xas, indices[j]);\n\txas_set_update(&xas, workingset_update_node);\n\n\tspin_lock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\n\txas_lock_irq(&xas);\n\n\txas_for_each(&xas, folio, indices[nr-1]) {\n\t\tif (xa_is_value(folio))\n\t\t\txas_store(&xas, NULL);\n\t}\n\n\txas_unlock_irq(&xas);\n\tif (mapping_shrinkable(mapping))\n\t\tinode_lru_list_add(mapping->host);\n\tspin_unlock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\nout:\n\tfolio_batch_remove_exceptionals(fbatch);\n}\n\n/**\n * folio_invalidate - Invalidate part or all of a folio.\n * @folio: The folio which is affected.\n * @offset: start of the range to invalidate\n * @length: length of the range to invalidate\n *\n * folio_invalidate() is called when all or part of the folio has become\n * invalidated by a truncate operation.\n *\n * folio_invalidate() does not have to release all buffers, but it must\n * ensure that no dirty buffer is left outside @offset and that no I/O\n * is underway against any of the blocks which are outside the truncation\n * point.  Because the caller is about to free (and possibly reuse) those\n * blocks on-disk.\n */\nvoid folio_invalidate(struct folio *folio, size_t offset, size_t length)\n{\n\tconst struct address_space_operations *aops = folio->mapping->a_ops;\n\n\tif (aops->invalidate_folio)\n\t\taops->invalidate_folio(folio, offset, length);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(folio_invalidate);\n\n/*\n * If truncate cannot remove the fs-private metadata from the page, the page\n * becomes orphaned.  It will be left on the LRU and may even be mapped into\n * user pagetables if we're racing with filemap_fault().\n *\n * We need to bail out if page->mapping is no longer equal to the original\n * mapping.  This happens a) when the VM reclaimed the page while we waited on\n * its lock, b) when a concurrent invalidate_mapping_pages got there first and\n * c) when tmpfs swizzles a page between a tmpfs inode and swapper_space.\n */\nstatic void truncate_cleanup_folio(struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tif (folio_mapped(folio))\n\t\tunmap_mapping_folio(folio);\n\n\tif (folio_needs_release(folio))\n\t\tfolio_invalidate(folio, 0, folio_size(folio));\n\n\t/*\n\t * Some filesystems seem to re-dirty the page even after\n\t * the VM has canceled the dirty bit (eg ext3 journaling).\n\t * Hence dirty accounting check is placed after invalidation.\n\t */\n\tfolio_cancel_dirty(folio);\n}\n\nint truncate_inode_folio(struct address_space *mapping, struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tif (folio->mapping != mapping)\n\t\treturn -EIO;\n\n\ttruncate_cleanup_folio(folio);\n\tfilemap_remove_folio(folio);\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic int try_folio_split_or_unmap(struct folio *folio, struct page *split_at,\n\t\t\t\t    unsigned long min_order)\n{\n\tenum ttu_flags ttu_flags =\n\t\tTTU_SYNC |\n\t\tTTU_SPLIT_HUGE_PMD |\n\t\tTTU_IGNORE_MLOCK;\n\tint ret;\n\n\tret = try_folio_split_to_order(folio, split_at, min_order);\n\n\t/*\n\t * If the split fails, unmap the folio, so it will be refaulted\n\t * with PTEs to respect SIGBUS semantics.\n\t *\n\t * Make an exception for shmem/tmpfs that for long time\n\t * intentionally mapped with PMDs across i_size.\n\t */\n\tif (ret && !shmem_mapping(folio->mapping)) {\n\t\ttry_to_unmap(folio, ttu_flags);\n\t\tWARN_ON(folio_mapped(folio));\n\t}\n\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\n/*\n * Handle partial folios.  The folio may be entirely within the\n * range if a split has raced with us.  If not, we zero the part of the\n * folio that's within the [start, end] range, and then split the folio if\n * it's large.  split_page_range() will discard pages which now lie beyond\n * i_size, and we rely on the caller to discard pages which lie within a\n * newly created hole.\n *\n * Returns false if splitting failed so the caller can avoid\n * discarding the entire folio which is stubbornly unsplit.\n */\nbool truncate_inode_partial_folio(struct folio *folio, loff_t start, loff_t end)\n{\n\tloff_t pos = folio_pos(folio);\n\tsize_t size = folio_size(folio);\n\tunsigned int offset, length;\n\tstruct page *split_at, *split_at2;\n\tunsigned int min_order;\n\n\tif (pos < start)\n\t\toffset = start - pos;\n\telse\n\t\toffset = 0;\n\tif (pos + size <= (u64)end)\n\t\tlength = size - offset;\n\telse\n\t\tlength = end + 1 - pos - offset;\n\n\tfolio_wait_writeback(folio);\n\tif (length == size) {\n\t\ttruncate_inode_folio(folio->mapping, folio);\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * We may be zeroing pages we're about to discard, but it avoids\n\t * doing a complex calculation here, and then doing the zeroing\n\t * anyway if the page split fails.\n\t */\n\tif (!mapping_inaccessible(folio->mapping))\n\t\tfolio_zero_range(folio, offset, length);\n\n\tif (folio_needs_release(folio))\n\t\tfolio_invalidate(folio, offset, length);\n\tif (!folio_test_large(folio))\n\t\treturn true;\n\n\tmin_order = mapping_min_folio_order(folio->mapping);\n\tsplit_at = folio_page(folio, PAGE_ALIGN_DOWN(offset) / PAGE_SIZE);\n\tif (!try_folio_split_or_unmap(folio, split_at, min_order)) {\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * try to split at offset + length to make sure folios within\n\t\t * the range can be dropped, especially to avoid memory waste\n\t\t * for shmem truncate\n\t\t */\n\t\tstruct folio *folio2;\n\n\t\tif (offset + length == size)\n\t\t\tgoto no_split;\n\n\t\tsplit_at2 = folio_page(folio,\n\t\t\t\tPAGE_ALIGN_DOWN(offset + length) / PAGE_SIZE);\n\t\tfolio2 = page_folio(split_at2);\n\n\t\tif (!folio_try_get(folio2))\n\t\t\tgoto no_split;\n\n\t\tif (!folio_test_large(folio2))\n\t\t\tgoto out;\n\n\t\tif (!folio_trylock(folio2))\n\t\t\tgoto out;\n\n\t\t/* make sure folio2 is large and does not change its mapping */\n\t\tif (folio_test_large(folio2) &&\n\t\t    folio2->mapping == (folio_test_dirty(folio))\n\t\treturn false;\n\ttruncate_inode_folio(folio->mapping, folio);\n\treturn true;\n}\n\n/*\n * Used to get rid of pages on hardware memory corruption.\n */\nint types like directories would need more auditing.\n\t */\n\tif (!S_ISREG(mapping->host->i_mode))\n\t\treturn -EIO;\n\treturn truncate_inode_folio(mapping, folio);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(generic_error_remove_folio);\n\n/**\n * mapping_evict_folio() - Remove an unused folio from the page-cache.\n * @mapping: The mapping this folio belongs to.\n * @folio: The folio to remove.\n *\n * Safely remove one folio from the page cache.\n * It only drops clean, unused folios.\n *\n * Context: Folio must be locked.\n * Return: The number of pages successfully removed.\n */\nlong mapping_evict_folio(struct address_space *mapping, struct folio *folio)\n{\n\t/* The page may have been truncated before it was locked */\n\tif (!mapping)\n\t\treturn 0;\n\tif (folio_test_dirty(folio) || folio_test_writeback(folio))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\t/* The refcount will be elevated if any page in the folio is mapped */\n\tif (folio_ref_count(folio) >\n\t\t\tfolio_nr_pages(folio) + folio_has_private(folio) + 1)\n\t\treturn 0;\n\tif (!filemap_release_folio(folio, 0))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\treturn remove_mapping(mapping, folio);\n}\n\n/**\n * truncate_inode_pages_range - truncate range of pages specified by start & end byte offsets\n * @mapping: mapping to truncate\n * @lstart: offset from which to truncate\n * @lend: offset to which to truncate (inclusive)\n *\n * Truncate the page cache, removing the pages that are between\n * specified offsets (and zeroing out partial pages\n * if lstart or lend + 1 is not page aligned).\n *\n * Truncate takes two passes - the first pass is nonblocking.  It will not\n * block on page locks and it will not block on writeback.  The second pass\n * will wait.  This is to prevent as much IO as possible in the affected region.\n * The first pass will remove most pages, so the search cost of the second pass\n * is low.\n *\n * We pass down the cache-hot hint to the page freeing code. Thus\n * mapping->nrpages can be non-zero when this function returns even after\n * truncation of the whole mapping.\n */\nvoid truncate_inode_pages(struct address_space *mapping, loff_t lstart)\n{\n\ttruncate_inode_pages_range(mapping, lstart, (loff_t)-1);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(truncate_inode_pages);\n\n/**\n * truncate_inode_pages_final - truncate *all* pages before inode dies\n * @mapping: mapping to truncate\n *\n * Called under (and serialized by) inode->i_rwsem.\n *\n * Filesystems have to use this in the .evict_inode path to inform the\n * VM that this is the final truncate and the inode is going away.\n */\nvoid truncate_inode_pages_final(struct address_space *mapping)\n{\n\t/*\n\t * Page reclaim can not participate in regular inode lifetime\n\t * management (can't call iput()) and thus can race with the\n\t * inode teardown.  Tell it when the address space is exiting,\n\t * so that it does not install eviction information after the\n\t * final truncate has begun.\n\t */\n\tmapping_set_exiting(mapping);\n\n\tif (!mapping_empty(mapping)) {\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * As truncation uses a lockless tree lookup, cycle\n\t\t * the tree lock to make sure any ongoing tree\n\t\t * modification that does not see AS_EXITING is\n\t\t * completed before starting the final truncate.\n\t\t */\n\t\txa_lock_irq(&mapping->i_pages);\n\t\txa_unlock_irq(&mapping->i_pages);\n\t}\n\n\ttruncate_inode_pages(mapping, 0);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(truncate_inode_pages_final);\n\n/**\n * mapping_try_invalidate - Invalidate all the evictable folios of one inode\n * @mapping: the address_space which holds the folios to invalidate\n * @start: the offset 'from' which to invalidate\n * @end: the offset 'to' which to invalidate (inclusive)\n * @nr_failed: How many folio invalidations failed\n *\n * This function is similar to invalidate_mapping_pages(), except that it\n * returns the number of folios which could not be evicted in @nr_failed.\n */\nunsigned long mapping_try_invalidate(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\tpgoff_t start, pgoff_t end, unsigned long *nr_failed)\n{\n\tpgoff_t indices[FOLIO_BATCH_SIZE];\n\tstruct folio_batch fbatch;\n\tpgoff_t index = start;\n\tunsigned long ret;\n\tunsigned long count = 0;\n\tint i;\n\n\tfolio_batch_init(&fbatch);\n\twhile (find_lock_entries(mapping, &index, end, &fbatch, indices)) {\n\t\tbool xa_has_values = false;\n\t\tint nr = folio_batch_count(&fbatch);\n\n\t\tfor (i = 0; i < nr; i++) {\n\t\t\tstruct folio *folio = fbatch.folios[i];\n\n\t\t\t/* We rely upon deletion not changing folio->index */\n\n\t\t\tif (xa_is_value(folio)) {\n\t\t\t\txa_has_values = true;\n\t\t\t\tcount++;\n\t\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t\t}\n\n\t\t\tret = mapping_evict_folio(mapping, folio);\n\t\t\tfolio_unlock(folio);\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * Invalidation is a hint that the folio is no longer\n\t\t\t * of interest and try to speed up its reclaim.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\tif (!ret) {\n\t\t\t\tdeactivate_file_folio(folio);\n\t\t\t\t/* Likely in the lru cache of a remote CPU */\n\t\t\t\tif (nr_failed)\n\t\t\t\t\t(*nr_failed)++;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\tcount += ret;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif (xa_has_values)\n\t\t\tclear_shadow_entries(mapping, indices[0], indices[nr-1]);\n\n\t\tfolio_batch_remove_exceptionals(&fbatch);\n\t\tfolio_batch_release(&fbatch);\n\t\tcond_resched();\n\t}\n\treturn count;\n}\n\n/**\n * invalidate_mapping_pages - Invalidate all clean, unlocked cache of one inode\n * @mapping: the address_space which holds the cache to invalidate\n * @start: the offset 'from' which to invalidate\n * @end: the offset 'to' which to invalidate (inclusive)\n *\n * This function removes pages that are clean, unmapped and unlocked,\n * as well as shadow entries.     "thought": "**Deep Dive into the `truncate_pagecache` Bug Fix**

Okay, so I've been dissecting this `truncate_pagecache` issue, and here's the breakdown of my thoughts as I went through the code. Initially, I was looking at the double `unmap_mapping_range` calls, and the comment confirmed that the first call is for efficiency, while the second is crucial for correctness. That makes sense, but it got me thinking about potential race conditions, particularly around folio mapping and truncation.

I considered the scenario where CPU 0 could establish a new mapping after the first `unmap_mapping_range` call but *before* `truncate_inode_pages` removes the folio. I traced the process, including `try_folio_split_or_unmap` calling `try_to_unmap`, confirming that any such mapping would be caught by either `truncate_inode_pages` zeroing and unmapping or the *second* `unmap_mapping_range` call.  This makes the filemap safe. I considered an order-0 folio completely beyond the new `i_size` and considered the `next_uptodate_folio` function, `max_idx`, and how that limits folio returns. This means any folio returned will always be straddling or intersecting the new size, so `filemap_map_pages` will correctly unmap/truncate it.

However, I realized the heart of the bug was a race condition in how `end_pgoff` was calculated. It's calculated based on the *old* `i_size` within `filemap_map_pages`, and *before* `next_uptodate_folio` runs. If the `i_size` shrinks between those two points, `end_pgoff` could become smaller than the folio's index (`xas.xa_index`). This, in turn, could cause an integer overflow in `nr_pages` calculation. Specifically, I went through the overflow calculation and saw how we end up with a huge number of pages being passed to `filemap_map_folio_range`! This leads to the corruption.

The fix, luckily, is simple: calculate `end_pgoff` *before* calling `next_uptodate_folio`. This then causes `xas_next_entry` to calculate based on that new value.  I then checked to see that the `xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff` condition is now *guaranteed*.

But that's not the end of it!  I then checked the new algorithm for calculating `nr_pages`, in a loop, inside `filemap_map_pages`. I had to confirm that `end_pgoff` does not change inside the loop. I also had to confirm that `xas.xa_index` is always increasing in the loop, that the logic in `next_uptodate_folio` guarantees `xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff`. However, it's possible for `i_size` to shrink *during* the loop. But even if it shrinks, `next_uptodate_folio` will skip the folio, and return `NULL` (terminating the loop).

Lastly, I made a deep dive into `filemap_map_folio_range`, to confirm that we don't accidentally map *too much* in this case. Even if we map the entire large folio because `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)`, it's safe because `truncate_pagecache` is going to split the folio, unmap the tail, zero the partial page, and drop it. Mapping the full folio is just fine because the truncation is waiting for the folio lock to drop. So any folio that maps beyond new `i_size` gets dropped.  And also `nr_pages` could map too far, and that's also ok, because truncation will occur. I then checked all the edge cases where `xas.xa_index` is used, and everything looks good!

In short, it's a textbook race condition, fixed by a simple code reordering that ensures the correct `i_size` is used throughout.

So, as long as `xas.xa_index <= end_pgoff` the loop calculates a correct number of pages for that folio.

Then, there is the edge case when the file is truncated to zero. In this case, `i_size_read(mapping->host)` returns 0. If that is the case, `file_end` becomes `ULONG_MAX`. This means that if we are attempting to map a file with a size of 0, `end_pgoff` is *not* clamped to zero, which is likely a bug.  The code uses `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1` which gives `ULONG_MAX`. If that's the case, then any `end_pgoff` is fine, which seems wrong. This is due to a race, where the folio is available, even though the file is of size 0! This allows a race with truncate to map PTEs that shouldn't be there.  This needs more investigation; it's possible that this is a subtle bug, where the `file_end` value is not consistently used with the file size.  The original code *also* had this bug.  We should *not* be mapping a folio to a file that is size 0. I suspect this is where the *real* problem lies. If the file is 0, we can not map a page! The logic of the patch doesn't handle this edge case, so the *existing* logic can still lead to memory corruption. I need to investigate what is meant to happen when a zero-size file triggers a page fault.  `do_fault_around` should probably have a check for the file size.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential Page Mapping Issue with Zero-Sized Files**

Okay, so I'm digging into how `filemap_map_pages` handles faults and page mapping, specifically with a focus on potential issues when the file size is zero. The typical `end_pgoff` passed to this function is calculated based on the VMA but is usually constrained by `do_fault_around`. I need to re-examine the logic in `filemap_map_pages`.

I'm noticing something: inside `filemap_map_pages`, there's this `file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1`. If `i_size` is zero, `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE)` becomes zero, and thus `file_end` becomes -1, which is `ULONG_MAX` (the maximum unsigned long value). Consequently, `end_pgoff` isn't clamped to zero or "no pages" when the file is empty. It's essentially kept at its original value, let's say 512.

Then, the code takes `rcu_read_lock()` and calls `next_uptodate_folio`. This might return a folio if there's still one in the page cache, perhaps due to a race with a concurrent truncation to size zero. Since `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`, the condition `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)` is always true. This can lead to a folio being mapped as a PMD! The internal loop calculates the number of pages to map: `nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1`.

If `end_pgoff` is 512, and the folio's index is zero, `nr_pages` becomes 1. This means a page is being mapped even though the file size is zero, which is wrong.

I'm wondering if this behavior existed before a specific patch. Pre-patch, the behavior seems the same: `file_end` would still become `ULONG_MAX` when `i_size` is zero, so the page is still mapped, or not really since this line is the same `end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);`. This confirms that the fault persists.

So, is `DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size, PAGE_SIZE) - 1` truly `ULONG_MAX` when `i_size` is zero? Yes, `0 - 1` underflows to become `ULONG_MAX`. If `file_end` is supposed to represent the highest valid page index, this is definitely wrong for a zero-sized file. There's no valid page index! I'm thinking, shouldn't there be a check for `i_size == 0`?

Hold on. `DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE)` returns the number of pages. If the size is zero, the number of pages is zero. Then `0 - 1` will be `ULONG_MAX`. Where else is `file_end` used?

I'm noticing that `file_end` also shows up in `do_fault_around`, this is the key. The function calculates `max_pgoff` using `min3`, but critically, the arguments are compared as unsigned long because they are `typeof(max_pgoff)`. If `i_size` is zero, `DIV_ROUND_UP(...) - 1` becomes `ULONG_MAX`. Thus, `max_pgoff` becomes `min(max_pgoff, pte_off)`, ignoring the file size. This means `do_fault_around` will call `filemap_map_pages` with `end_pgoff` up to the end of the VMA! It maps pages beyond the file's end when `i_size` is zero.

I wonder, if `i_size` is zero, why are there still pages in the page cache? Perhaps a concurrent truncate is happening, or there's a bug in the page cache management. If a truncate is in progress, `next_uptodate_folio` can find a page that's about to be removed. We map it, but then `truncate_pagecache` will come along and unmap it using `unmap_mapping_range()` because the truncate lock, is not held during page fault, only the folio lock is. The pages get mapped and unmapped because of the concurrency. Let me look at `next_uptodate_folio` and confirm if it actually locks the folio.

If `end_pgoff` is indeed `ULONG_MAX`, then `xas.xa_index <= ULONG_MAX` is always true, which makes `nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1`. The problem arises if `i_size` decreases concurrently between when we *enter* `filemap_map_pages` and the point at which we calculate `file_end`.  This is because `file_end` can then become smaller than `xas.xa_index` (which was derived from the *original* `end_pgoff`), and therefore `end_pgoff` could become less than `xas.xa_index` after the `min()` operation.

Inside the loop, the `nr_pages` calculation goes like this: `nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1;`.  If `end_pgoff` is less than `xas.xa_index`, then `min(end, end_pgoff)` will also be less than `xas.xa_index`. That results in a negative value for `nr_pages`, which then underflows to a large number, which then gets passed to `filemap_map_folio_range()`.

The fix of moving the `end_pgoff` calculation *before* `next_uptodate_folio()` looks sensible because `end_pgoff` will be bounded by `file_end` *before* we start iterating.

But hold on... `i_size` is read locklessly, which means the value can change concurrently! `file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;` This concerns me. What happens if `file_end` changes *after* we've read its value?

If `i_size` expands, our `file_end` will be smaller, leading us to map fewer pages (up to the old size), which is fine. Concurrent writes are allowed to expand a file after we've started a fault.  However, if `i_size` shrinks due to a truncation, the `truncate_pagecache()` function unmaps and removes folios from the page cache under the mapping lock.

But wait a minute! `filemap_map_pages` is called during a fault-around operation! This is `do_fault_around`. What actually prevents the file from being truncated *after* we've read `file_end`?

Suppose `file_end` is read as 100 pages, so we clamp `end_pgoff` to 99. Then, `next_uptodate_folio()` returns a folio at index 50. Now, imagine a concurrent thread truncates the file to 10 pages. The concurrent thread will unmap and remove folios from index 10 onwards. Our original thread continues, thinking `file_end` is still 99.  It proceeds to map the folio at index 50 into the page tables!  *Is mapping a truncated folio even valid*? I don't think so.

Ah, in `filemap_map_pages`, we acquire `folio_trylock(folio)`. But where exactly does this occur? `next_uptodate_folio` does not call `folio_trylock()`, it calls `folio_try_get()`! I need to double-check `next_uptodate_folio` to confirm where `folio_trylock()` is called.

Okay, let's break this down. That initial `if (unlikely(xas->xa_index >= max)) return xas_find(xas, max);` is the key. It looks like it calls `xas_find`, which is the function that determines whether a folio will be returned or not. It supports `xas->xa_index == max`, but returns `NULL` if `xas->xa_index > max`. I was initially worried that `next_uptodate_folio` could return a folio causing `xas.xa_index` to exceed `end_pgoff`.

So, say we have `xas.xa_index == 99` and `end_pgoff == 99`. `xas_find(&xas, 99)` would find a folio at index 99, and `xas.xa_index` becomes 99. The loop calculates `nr_pages` as `min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1`. Because `end >= 99`, this simplifies to `99 - 99 + 1 = 1`.

Now, the concern was, what about the *next* iteration? `next_uptodate_folio` calls `xas_next_entry(&xas, 99)`. Crucially,  `xas_find` handles the case where it's *already* at an entry. The comments are very important here. The next entry index becomes >= 100. That means `xas_find` will check `xas->xa_index > max`. Since the next index is now greater than max, `xas_find` will return `NULL`.

So, it seems that `xas_find` *prevents* `xas.xa_index` from ever exceeding `end_pgoff`. The `nr_pages` calculation won't underflow. Phew. That's a relief, it should be impossible for this to happen.

However, the loop in `filemap_map_pages` has a loop! If `folio_test_large(folio)` is true, we call `filemap_map_folio_range`, which has its own logic.  I need to check this next.

1.  `do_fault_around` explicitly uses PMD alignment or `fault_around_pages`. Consequently, the `[start_pgoff, end_pgoff]` range resides entirely within one PTE table. Therefore, `vmf->pte += ...` will never traverse a page table boundary. This part is sound.

2.  The calculation of `file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;` is problematic. If `i_size` is zero, `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE)` results in 0. Subtracting 1 from 0, since `pgoff_t` is an unsigned long, leads to `ULONG_MAX`.

3.  The subsequent line, `end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);`, doesn't clamp `end_pgoff` if `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`, which is exactly what happens when `i_size` is zero, it essentially disables the clamp.

4.  If `i_size` is 0 initially, `mapping->i_pages` *could* have pages if the truncate process isn't complete yet. The `next_uptodate_folio` function can still find folios in such a case. The check within `next_uptodate_folio` for `max_idx = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE);` ensures that it returns NULL if `xas->xa_index >= max_idx`. If `i_size` is indeed 0, `max_idx` will be 0, and folios are skipped, if `i_size` remains zero.

    "thought": "**Analysis of `filemap_map_pages()` with Patch**

Okay, so I've been tracing through this `filemap_map_pages()` function, specifically looking at a patch that moved some calculations outside an RCU read-side critical section. Initially, my focus was on whether the patch addressed a potential integer overflow issue when calculating the number of pages to map. I confirmed that the original code, by calculating the file size and the `end_pgoff` *after* the initial call to `next_uptodate_folio`, had a window where an incorrect `end_pgoff` could lead to a massive `nr_pages` value due to an underflow, subsequently causing a buffer overflow when passed to the function `filemap_map_folio_range`, and ultimately corrupting page tables. The patch fixed this by calculating the `end_pgoff` *before* the first `next_uptodate_folio` call.

However, moving those calculations out of the RCU critical section raised concerns about concurrency issues, specifically a potential TOCTOU (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use) vulnerability. If the file size changes *after* the `end_pgoff` calculation, but *before* the mapping occurs, stale data could be mapped.

I then started thinking about the interaction with `truncate_pagecache`, which shrinks file sizes. After tracing the logic, it seems that the check within `next_uptodate_folio` for file size after locking the folio correctly prevents us from mapping pages beyond the new EOF because it rechecks `i_size_read(mapping->host)` during the RCU read-side critical section.

So it seemed the patch was correct. It fixes a bug without introducing a new one. I was checking if there was any locking issue within `rcu_read_lock()` that would affect concurrency. Nothing!

    "thought": "**Analyzing Page Mapping in `filemap_map_pages`**

Okay, let's break this down systematically. I'm focusing on the reference counting and memory management within `filemap_map_pages`. I'm starting from the premise that `do_set_pmd` is the key. It looks like `do_set_pmd` calls `spin_lock(vmf->ptl)` internally, and that's good – no sleeps, so we're safe from deadlocks.

I see `add_mm_counter` at the end of the loop, plus `pte_unmap_unlock`. Then `trace_mm_filemap_map_pages`, which is safe under the RCU read lock. Good.

Now, a crucial point: if `next_uptodate_folio` returns NULL, we `goto out;`. And `out:` does an `rcu_read_unlock()`. Correct.

Now I'm diving into the loop. If `vmf->pte` is NULL, we bail out via `goto out;`, which is protected by the RCU lock. Correct.

Okay, let's dig into the core loop. It starts with `folio_type = mm_counter_file(folio);` and then the main logic. The crucial branch is `filemap_map_pmd`. If it returns true, we hit `VM_FAULT_NOPAGE` and `goto out;`.

I need to understand what `filemap_map_pmd` does *if* it succeeds. It calls `do_set_pmd`. Now, here's the kicker: `filemap_map_pmd` *doesn't* call `folio_put(folio)` if `do_set_pmd` is successful. This is important! So, what does `do_set_pmd` actually *do* with the folio's reference count?

I see `folio_get(folio)` in `try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp`, but in `do_set_pmd`, I have to be careful. I remember `next_uptodate_folio` does a `folio_try_get(folio)`. Okay, so `folio` starts with an extra reference in the general case. So, does `do_set_pmd` then consume it?

Looking at `do_set_pmd`, there is no `folio_put`, even on success! The success path returns with `spin_unlock(vmf->ptl)`, but no decrement of the reference count.
So, if `do_set_pmd` maps a PMD, *it doesn't release the folio*! That must mean it's taking ownership of the reference somehow.

So, how does the reference management work when a folio is mapped into the page cache, and then mapped to a PTE/PMD? The page cache holds a reference initially, and when it is mapped to a PTE or PMD it does not *necessarily* hold a reference... Wait, it should use `folio_ref_add` or similar.

I see `folio_ref_add` in `filemap_map_folio_range`. Aha! It uses the `ref_from_caller` variable. This is critical. `ref_from_caller` is initialized to 1, indicating that it consumes a reference from the caller. Thus it *does* consume the reference. That reference originated from `next_uptodate_folio`.

What happens with `do_set_pmd`? I must now go search the function for `folio_ref_add` or similar.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the PMD and Folio Handling in File Mapping and Truncation**

My initial focus was on understanding how `do_set_pmd` manages reference counts, specifically whether it consumes a reference from the caller. The code reveals that `do_set_pmd` indeed increments the folio's reference count by `HPAGE_PMD_NR - 1`, effectively consuming the original reference and adding 511 more. This behavior is crucial when the mapping is a huge page. I then considered potential failure scenarios within `do_set_pmd`, specifically the `VM_FAULT_FALLBACK` return, and how this impacts the reference count. The logic in `filemap_map_pmd` demonstrates that when `do_set_pmd` fails (returns `VM_FAULT_FALLBACK`), the reference isn't consumed, and the code falls back to handling PTEs, ensuring the balance of references.  I confirmed there's no problem with holding `rcu_read_lock()` while checking certain PMD flags, as page table locks are spinlocks.

The core of the investigation then shifted to a patch related to file truncation and its potential to introduce a Time-of-Check-to-Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerability. The patch modifies how `end_pgoff` is calculated and used to limit the mapping range. Initially, I noted that the patch ensures `end_pgoff` is calculated only once, aiming to mitigate a race condition. However, I delved deeper and discovered that `next_uptodate_folio` calculates `max_idx` again, potentially leading to another TOCTOU scenario if the file size changes between the initial `end_pgoff` calculation and this subsequent `max_idx` calculation. This would impact the returned folio. If the file shrinks, the code might return a folio for a region that's no longer within the file bounds.

I examined scenarios where a file might shrink while a folio is being mapped.  The code calculates `nr_pages` based on an outdated `end_pgoff` (before the file shrink). This could lead to mapping pages beyond the new EOF.  Even before the patch this race condition existed. Then I carefully considered the impact of a concurrent `truncate` operation, specifically the interaction between `unmap_mapping_range` (which removes PTEs), and `truncate_inode_pages` (which zero's the tail pages). If `filemap_map_pages` installs PTEs for pages beyond EOF, and then `truncate` occurs, the existing PTEs are *not* removed during `truncate_inode_pages` because `unmap_mapping_range` has already ran and the PTEs may not even exist yet. The pages are instead zeroed and the folios is truncated.

This raises a critical question about "SIGBUS semantics". By leaving PTEs mapped beyond EOF, accesses to those pages (now zeroed) would *not* trigger a SIGBUS, violating expected behavior. I looked at `filemap_fault` to confirm that accessing such pages *should* return VM_FAULT_SIGBUS. However, if the pages are mapped preventatively, they'll simply return zeroes.

The lock ordering between `filemap_map_pages` and `truncate_inode_pages` are crucial to understand. `filemap_map_pages` has a folio trylock. `truncate_inode_pages` must lock a folio. The key race condition occurs: a race when both have an established PTE to the now-smaller file. `filemap_map_pages` maps the folio. The `unmap_mapping_range` happens first. Then the folio is locked and the tail of the folio is truncated. The PTEs installed by `filemap_map_pages` can remain.

I realized the check for `i_size` happens *before* a folio is returned, but the crucial problem is `filemap_map_pages` calculating `end_pgoff` before any folio is returned. If truncation happens after this calculation, pages beyond the new file size may be mapped.

There's no complete protection. `do_fault_around` which calls `filemap_map_pages` is under `mmap_read_lock`. The `truncate` holds `i_rwsem` and does *not* hold `mmap_lock`. So truncation can run concurrently to `do_fault_around` leading to the TOCTOU.

I then considered whether partial folio truncation leads to `unmap_mapping_range` calls, but it doesn't.

    "thought": "**File Mapping Conundrums and Potential SIGBUS Violations**

Okay, so looking at how `truncate_pagecache` works, I see that the double call to `unmap_mapping_range` is definitely intentional to handle concurrent access beyond EOF and is indeed safe, which is a relief. No stale PTEs, perfect.

Now, about RCU, my concern was whether there were RCU-protected objects being freed while within `rcu_read_lock()`. The `next_uptodate_folio` function relies on RCU because of the `xas_next_entry`. The call to `folio_free_rcu`, via `filemap_remove_folio` and then `folio_put` isn't a problem, because of the `folio_try_get` when calling `next_uptodate_folio`. If `folio_try_get` returns a folio, it won't be freed while the folio is in use. So far, so good.

But then I saw the `filemap_map_pages` function, where `folio_put` *is* called within an `rcu_read_lock()` section, so it's essential that `folio_put` does not sleep. It doesn't, it just drops the refcount. I was getting ahead of myself, but it's important to remember that `folio_unlock`, which uses `clear_bit_unlock`, also *does not* sleep. The only reason for the read lock around `filemap_map_pages` is to keep `xas` valid, since `next_uptodate_folio` which uses `xas_next_entry` relies on RCU, so that makes sense.

Now for the *real* problem. I was looking into how `end_pgoff` underflows in `filemap_map_pages`. I see `DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(...), PAGE_SIZE) - 1`. If `i_size` is zero, we get `ULONG_MAX`! This means if `i_size` is zero, then grows, `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`. If `i_size` becomes 0, and then non-zero, the program will calculate `file_end` as `ULONG_MAX`, and the function would then proceed as if the file is arbitrarily large. Now, the `next_uptodate_folio` function will likely return a folio regardless (given the new non-zero `i_size`). But, *if* the folio has grown beyond that i_size, then we'll map that folio, including pages beyond `i_size`, as a *PMD*, because `file_end >= folio_next_index` will be true. That means the `filemap_map_pmd` fast path might be taken!

That's a HUGE problem, because the code *specifically* says not to map across `i_size` with PMDs to preserve SIGBUS semantics! Imagine a scenario where `i_size` is 0, a fault occurs, then `i_size` grows to a single page, but a huge folio (order 9, 512 pages) is found. Then we will map the huge folio as a PMD! *That* violates the comment's intent. Because of a race condition! If the CPU reads the old `i_size` *before* it's updated, it calculates `file_end` as `ULONG_MAX`. After it is updated, but still within the RCU read lock, `filemap_map_pmd` is called, and we map a PMD that is MUCH larger than the new `i_size`. That's a clear SIGBUS violation and a real bug.

Consequently, `end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end)` leaves `end_pgoff` unrestricted. If a concurrent thread extends the file size and instantiates a large folio just before `next_uptodate_folio()` is called, the loop will retrieve that folio (because its index is now valid against the new `i_size`). However, `filemap_map_pages` continues using `file_end = ULONG_MAX`. The condition `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)` will incorrectly evaluate to true, allowing `filemap_map_pmd()` or the fast-path in `filemap_map_folio_range()` to map the entire large folio into the page tables. This maps pages beyond the actual new EOF without triggering a SIGBUS on access. Before this patch, if `i_size` was 0, `next_uptodate_folio()` would simply return NULL, exiting the function before `file_end` was ever evaluated."

Okay, here's the breakdown of my thinking. I've been digging into this patch, and I've zeroed in on a significant issue related to how file mappings are handled, particularly in the face of concurrent file size changes. It all boils down to an integer underflow that can lead to bypassing SIGBUS protection.

The core of the problem stems from this patch's alteration to the file mapping logic: The calculation of `file_end` (which represents the last valid page offset for the file) was moved before the call to `next_uptodate_folio`. In the original code, if the file size was zero, `next_uptodate_folio` would return null, and `file_end` would never even be calculated. This meant the underflow would never occur.

However, after the patch, the `file_end` calculation now happens unconditionally. If `i_size` (the file's size) is zero *at the moment* this calculation takes place, `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1` underflows, resulting in `file_end` becoming `ULONG_MAX` (the largest possible unsigned long value).

Now, here's where the race condition comes into play: *if*, after `file_end` is calculated to `ULONG_MAX`, *another thread* subsequently increases the file size (perhaps via `ftruncate` or a write), then the subsequent call to `next_uptodate_folio` *will* find a valid folio in the page cache because `i_size` is now larger than 0. The code then proceeds as though `file_end` is a valid, unrestricted upper bound.

This is a problem! Here's why. The following conditions evaluate to TRUE:

1. `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)`: Because `file_end` is ULONG_MAX, and `folio_next_index()` is usually something small like 512, this is always TRUE.
2. `folio_within_vma()` is true because the faulting page is within the VMAs address space.
3. `(addr0 & PMD_MASK) == ((addr0 + folio_size(folio) - 1) & PMD_MASK)` meaning that we will map the entire folio and potentially map more bytes than EOF.

What this translates to is that the subsequent call to `filemap_map_pmd` or `filemap_map_folio_range` is now allowed to map an entire *PMD-sized folio* (or even a larger folio!) into the page table, *even though the file itself is now, for example, only one page*. This can lead to the mapping of potentially hundreds or thousands of pages beyond the actual EOF of the file. Pages 1..511 are valid after that concurrent expand. Accesses to these pages will *not* trigger the expected SIGBUS signal because they are now valid entries in the page table, even though they should be out-of-bounds!

This bypasses the expected protection against accessing invalid pages and is a violation of SIGBUS semantics.

The original code avoided the underflow by not computing `file_end` when `i_size` was zero and now the vulnerability exists *only* when `i_size` is 0, AND is grown concurrently. It is a brilliant time-of-check-to-time-of-use (TOCTOU) and integer underflow bug, caused by moving `file_end` computation before the folio lock. It is critical that this is addressed.
